sentences.text: I'm Renate Loeser, L-O-E-S-E-R.
Is Rossmere, R-O-S-S-M-E-R-E.
No.
It's March 6, 1924.
In Hamburg, Germany.
My family consisted of my mother.
Her name was Kate Loeser.
My father, Oscar Loeser.
And my brother, Oswald Loeser.
He was five years younger than I.
My father was a merchant.
He was also a partner in a chemical firm.
Life was very comfortable.
I had a very nice childhood.
I had very loving parents, parents who loved each other.
And so it was a very good family life.
It was-- I cannot think of any incidents that, that marred that my life at that point.
I had a lovely childhood.
My grandparents lived about 15, 20 minutes away.
It was-- people rarely had cars at that point.
So when we visited, we walked to them.
And I remember many times walking with my mother to visit the grandparents.
Maternal grandparents.
And my paternal-- sorry.
Agnes and Richard Shaeffer-- S-C-H-A-E-F-E-R.
You're welcome.
They lived five minutes from my maternal grandparents.
They lived in the back of them.
And their name, my grandmother's name was Marta Loeser.
And her husband was Emil Loeser.
We did.
My maternal grandparents were quite social and gave many parties, and I remember well.
It was a lot of fun we had at those parties, even though I was very young.
I'm afraid it wasn't celebrated at all.
We got brought up totally irreligious and barely knew that we were Jewish.
We knew we were Jewish, but in no way did we celebrate the holidays.
We celebrated Christmas as a family holiday, and that was about it.
Through my parents telling us.
We were-- and later on, of course, through Hitler, we became, I became more aware of it.
But at the time, there was very little talk about religion, though.
It meant we're just like everybody else.
And it really didn't mean very much to me, I'm sorry to say, because, simply because there was no religion in, in our household.
We were-- the important thing was to be a good human being.
Yes.
Paternal, yes.
Both were Jewish.
Yes.
My maternal grandmother, I think, came from a very religious household.
I think she was born in Denmark.
But there was very little talk about that.
So I know very little about it, nothing really.
Nothing.
No.
It did when I was about eight, nine-- nine years old.
And simply because I had a girlfriend who lived in the same floor we did in our apartment building.
And she was Christian.
And they were a large family of lovely people.
And they moved away into the suburbs of Hamburg and [GERMAN] And when I visited there once, several times, I became very much aware of that I was somehow different than they were.
For one thing, their servants all commented on the wonderful hair I had, which was totally different than my friends, who was very blonde and had straight hair.
And mine was extremely curly and dark brown.
And I was wondering at that point why was she pointing out my hair so?
What were-- why was it so different?
I'd never thought of it before.
And later on, I realized why she had done that.
And there was a lot of talk at the dinner table about Jews and Christians, so on.
And the worst of it was that in the neighborhood there was a group of teenage boys.
And when they heard that I came to visit the family, they threatened me-- to beat me up.
And there was a lovely festival where you walk with a lantern, which has a light in it, a candle in it.
And you walk at night in the procession through the neighborhood and sing songs, and so on.
And they said that when we-- indeed this was going to take place and we were going to walk through the woods with our lanterns that they were going to come and beat me up.
And I was very frightened by that because I was Jewish.
And I was very frightened by that, but I didn't tell it to anybody that this was so.
My girlfriend knew it, but didn't say anything.
So I wasn't going to wait till this happened, and so I faked a terrible stomachache.
And so much so that the doctor came and couldn't find anything wrong, naturally.
But my friend's mother thought it better to send me home because of the stomachache, and so I escaped the beating up, which I might or not have gotten.
But it was very scary.
And that, for the first time, made me aware that indeed I was different than others, than this good friend of mine, perhaps.
Did it end your relationship with this friend.
Yes, it kind of petered out.
For one thing, she had moved away.
But then several years later, maybe two years later, we met her in the city.
And we were-- my mother and I were shopping for clothes.
And we met this friend and she pretended she did not know me anymore.
It was kind of devastating.
This was in Hamburg.
That was outside of Hamburg.
Yes, where they lived then.
They moved.
Well, let's say.
In my experience, that's where it had seemed to be more.
No, no, not until the Kristallnacht.
Before that, his business was also in the suburbs of Hamburg, in [GERMAN] And you had to take a train in order to get there.
So I don't-- as far as I know, I don't think he encountered any unpleasantnesses.
Before that night, my parents sent me to a public school so that I would meet everybody and be educated together with everybody.

And in the later years, in '33, I noticed that some of the boys were wearing a uniform, a Hitler Youth uniform, and that they wear absent a lot from school because they had to participate in the Hitler Youth meetings.
But there was basically no anti-Semitism at that point.
I didn't feel in any way shut out.
And so--
No, the teacher was wonderful.
He was a young new teacher who was very, very lovely.
His name was Herr Puck, P-U-C-K.
And we all adored him, boys and girls.
That was a totally mixed class.
And he handled it, seemed to have handled it very well.
So that, that-- but that was the, really the very first awareness of, of the Hitler Youth and things to come.
It's a good question.
Maybe a little bit, yes.
Yes, there were some.
Not that I remember.
There may have been.
But I-- I don't remember that.
Perhaps a little bit, yes.
Yes, it was something you really didn't talk about very much.
Not very much actually.
I think my parents talked more amongst themselves about what was happening.
And at the dinner table, I heard the words of, you know, what might be difficult times ahead.
[SIGHS] Very little, really, very little.
Well, at the very end, yes.
But gradually.
When I was 10, I was sent to a private girl school.
In order to get a better education than I had received up to then.
And-- and this school closed in 1937 because of so many Jewish children-- girls-- having left and immigrated to other countries that they couldn't keep it up.
But it was not exclusively for Jewish.
It wasn't exclusively, but there was a great many.
The percentage was fairly high of Jewish girls.
Yes
Right.
And there were other things.
We had different religions instruction.
I had to go to the temple for my religious instructions, while the Christian girls received theirs in school.
One day, I remember we were-- the school closed because Hitler was going to visit Hamburg and was coming past, riding past our school.
And so the school closed in order to make it possible for everyone to view this.
And the Jewish girls were sent home because they weren't supposed to participate.
But I decided that I wanted to see what this was all about.
And so I stayed and watched him coming past in the car, standing up.
I just saw a person standing up in the car, raising his hands, and that's it.
Not too far away.
It's hard-- hard to remember exactly.
But I could see him quite, quite clearly.
A little bit because of the importance this all had, the whole school closing and everybody out there watching this person, this one person, coming by and what would he do.
No, none.
As a matter of fact, the teacher, my last year's teacher, was a very, was a woman who was a Nazi, really.
And so there never was any discussion.
The less discussion, the better.
She taught us, but we did not know of her.
I mean, we knew of her private feelings.
But it was not mentioned in school.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It's a difficult question.
Because there was so little from home that made us feel really Jewish, you see.
Perhaps.
More confusion than definite feelings of being Jewish.
It felt good being in the religious instructions in the temple and going to attending some of the holidays.
But it was as being in a vacuum because my parents weren't there, my parents never set foot in a, in a synagogue or a temple.
So it was-- my brother didn't.
So it was just me being there, somewhere, learning something that the others hadn't learned or didn't know very much about.
Why then did your parents send you?
Their idea was to offer us the possibilities of choosing the religion we wanted to when-- once we were old enough to do so.
And so they wanted to show me what the Jewish religion was like, and so thereby had me take the religious instruction.
It was about the Bible, yes, and about the holy holidays-- some.
I don't think so because we viewed this as being a little bit of a nuisance, also, you see.
I mean, to go there on a Saturday afternoon or Saturday morning.
Something.
But, on the other hand, I also felt a little bit of a waste of time, because I think it is very important that it's encouraged at home and talked about, yes.
Well, I was there from 10 till 13, 12 or 13.
And then I had one year of private tutoring with some other children whose school had closed, some of my own classmates.
Yes.
All Jewish, yes.
And that was wonderful.
I learned more in that year than I did up to then in the entire school year, I think.
It was just marvelous.
We had various teachers and very dedicated, very knowledgeable people.
It was marvelous.
Yes.
Yes.
Absolutely.
And at home we were not allowed to have any non-Jewish servants and had to be very careful in that.
And we were not allowed to sit on park benches, I remember, not allowed to talk to anyone who's not Jewish.
It became quite restricted.
But it was-- yes, go ahead.
Yes, I'm sure he was.
That was not discussed, or it was discussed in the privacy of [? that. ?]
Well, it was the same with them.
My grandpa-- father-- the factory belonged to him.
And so he was affected by it just the same way my father was.
And that all stopped on the 11th of November during the Kristallnacht.
Well, my experience on the night was nothing.
I went to sleep and didn't know about anything.
But the next morning when my father got out of the train at the station in [GERMAN], he was taken by SS people and incarcerated in a concentration camp, together was two other partners.
Yes, one was my uncle, maternal uncle, Erwin Shaeffer.
And one was a Mr.
Levy, and he was the engineer.
It was Oranienburg.
It with near Berlin.
And then that of course was a devastating moment.
He was there three or four months.
He was very lucky.
Through my mother-- got word.
How, I don't know.
She talked to us.
And she mainly talked to us in the-- I remember-- in the bathroom and while the water was running.
See, she was very afraid that people would hear.
And so that was the way she was communicating with us.
We always had to come to the bathroom.
I remember the water was running.
No, Jews, period, had to pay millions of dollars-- millions of marks, rather.
For the government, just because they're Jewish.
They weren't allowed to have any money.
The condition to get my father out, I believe, was that he could leave the country at that point.
That was still possible.
And so I-- my mother's sister lived in England and was able to get us all, save all our lives.
Her name was Lotte Elkon, E-L-K-A-N.
She lived in London, yes, in [INAUDIBLE].
She vouched for all of us.
She gave us the affidavits.
And first she vouched my brother and myself so that we could come over.
It was a children's transport.
This was December 1938.
It was the 15th of December, 1938.
No, he was still interned, he was still away, incarcerated.
No, no.
Nothing, none.
He never told me about it.
No, he talked a little bit to my brother but never to my mother and myself about it.
It must have been devastating [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for him.
No, you see, my mother decided to send us away when-- because she just didn't know what was going to happen after my father was taken by the Nazis.
And I think it was a very courageous move on her part and must have been tearing her heart to pieces because I know what it would feel like if I'd had to send my children away.
I was 14 and my brother was 9.
Not very long.
You see, the 11th of November was Kristallnacht.
And we were shipped away on the 15th of December.
So it's barely, it's a little bit over a month.
And--
That she prepared us for this move.
It was organized by a Jewish organization who organized the Kindertransport.
Don't ask me for the name, I'm not quite sure of it.
And you had to apply for this, and, I imagine, pay quite a bit of money.
I not-- wasn't sure of the details.
I Was 14, you really are still very immature and--
Well, to say that we would live with my aunt, that life would be a lot different, and to try and make life as easy as possible for her and be good children, in other words.
And that they would try and return as soon as possible and come that way too as soon as possible, as soon as my father would be released.
But of course, nobody knew when that would be.
Or if that would be.
Or if that would be.
That's right.
Well, a lot of children did.

The evening before we left, of course, a lot of relatives and friends came to say goodbye and brought lovely presents, which then we couldn't take along because we were only allowed to a little suitcase to take with us.
And as far as I know, it was only one-- one mark or 10 marks, probably 10 marks.
I remember one mark, but I think that would be too little.
It could have been.
And on the morning of the transport when we were supposed to go to the station, my brother had a terrible stomachache.
And so wife my mother called a doctor, because our doctor no longer was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Hamburg.
And she called a Dr.
Stone, who later became my stepfather-in-law.
And he prescribed some little medication in a little bottle.
And he told me to keep this bottle in my pocketbook and give my brother some of that medication from time to time.
